skill.name: sample-size-power-calculator
skill.description: Advanced sample size and power calculations for complex study designs including survival analysis, clustered designs, and multiple comparisons.
skill.summary: Statistical power and sample size calculator for advanced research study designs.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 82 static analysis findings are false positives. The scanner incorrectly flagged statistical function calls (scipy.stats.norm.ppf) as weak cryptographic algorithms, markdown documentation examples as shell command execution, and standard text strings as reconnaissance patterns. The skill contains a legitimate Python CLI tool that performs sample size calculations using numpy and scipy with no network access, credential handling, or file I/O operations. The legacy audit result JSON confirms this skill passed all security gates.

content.user_title: Calculate Sample Size and Statistical Power for Research Studies
content.value_statement: Researchers struggle with complex sample size calculations for advanced study designs. This skill automates power analysis for t-tests, ANOVA, survival analysis, chi-square tests, and non-inferiority trials with built-in dropout adjustments.
actual_capabilities: Calculates sample size for independent and paired t-tests using Cohen's d effect size
Computes sample size for survival analysis using the Schoenfeld formula
Determines sample size for chi-square tests, ANOVA, and non-inferiority trials
Generates power curve data across a range of effect sizes
Adjusts sample size estimates for expected dropout rates
Supports configurable significance levels, power thresholds, and allocation ratios
limitations: Uses normal approximation formulas which may be less accurate for very small sample sizes
Does not support Bayesian power analysis or simulation-based approaches
Limited to pre-specified test types and cannot handle custom statistical models
Does not calculate actual power from existing data, only prospective power analysis

use_cases.title: Clinical Trial Planning
use_cases.description: A researcher planning a clinical trial needs to determine the minimum sample size required to detect a clinically meaningful effect with adequate statistical power.

use_cases.title: Thesis Research Design
use_cases.description: A graduate student designing a thesis study needs power analysis for ANOVA with multiple groups to justify their proposed sample size to an ethics committee.

use_cases.title: Study Design Comparison
use_cases.description: A biostatistician evaluating different study design options needs power curves across a range of effect sizes to recommend the most efficient approach.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Sample Size Calculation
prompt_templates.prompt: Calculate the sample size needed for an independent t-test with effect size 0.5, alpha 0.05, and power 0.8.
prompt_templates.scenario: A beginner researcher needs a simple sample size for a two-group comparison study.

prompt_templates.title: Survival Analysis Power Calculation
prompt_templates.prompt: Calculate the sample size for a survival study with a hazard ratio of 0.7, alpha 0.05, power 0.9, and an expected event rate of 60 percent.
prompt_templates.scenario: An intermediate user designing a clinical trial with time-to-event outcomes.

prompt_templates.title: Non-Inferiority Trial with Dropout
prompt_templates.prompt: Calculate the sample size for a non-inferiority trial with a margin of 0.2, sigma 0.5, alpha 0.025, power 0.8, and adjust for a 15 percent expected dropout rate.
prompt_templates.scenario: An experienced researcher planning a non-inferiority study with attrition considerations.

prompt_templates.title: Power Curve for ANOVA Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a power curve for an ANOVA with 4 groups across effect sizes from 0.1 to 0.8 with alpha 0.05 and power 0.8. Show the sample size for each effect size level.
prompt_templates.scenario: An expert user comparing statistical power across multiple design options to justify study feasibility.

output_examples.input: Calculate sample size for independent t-test with effect size 0.5, alpha 0.05, power 0.8, and equal group allocation.
output_examples.output: The required sample size for an independent t-test with Cohen's d of 0.5, alpha of 0.05, and power of 0.8 is 64 participants per group for a total of 128 participants. With a 20 percent dropout adjustment, the final target is 160 participants.

output_examples.input: Calculate sample size for survival analysis with hazard ratio 0.7, alpha 0.05, power 0.8, and event probability 0.5.
output_examples.output: Using the Schoenfeld formula, the survival analysis requires 66 total events to detect a hazard ratio of 0.7 with 80 percent power. The total sample size needed is approximately 132 participants (66 per group), assuming a 50 percent event probability.

output_examples.input: Generate power curve for independent t-test across effect sizes from 0.2 to 0.8.
output_examples.output: Power curve shows the required sample size decreasing as effect size increases. At effect size 0.2 the study needs 788 participants per group. At effect size 0.5 it needs 128 participants total. At effect size 0.8 it needs 52 participants total. All calculations use alpha 0.05 and power 0.8.

best_practices: Always specify the expected effect size based on published literature or pilot studies rather than choosing arbitrary values
Include dropout rate adjustments when planning longitudinal studies to ensure adequate power at study completion
Document all statistical assumptions including significance level, desired power, and allocation ratio in the study protocol
anti_patterns: Using underpowered studies to draw definitive conclusions from non-significant results without acknowledging insufficient power
Adjusting effect size after calculation to achieve a desired sample size instead of designing a feasible study
Running multiple underpowered subgroup analyses without correction for multiple comparisons

faq.question: What effect size should I use for my calculation?
faq.answer: Use effect sizes from published literature in your field, pilot study data, or established minimal clinically important differences. Cohen's d of 0.2 is small, 0.5 is medium, and 0.8 is large.

faq.question: Can this calculator handle clustered or multi-level designs?
faq.answer: The calculator does not directly support clustered or multi-level designs. You would need a design effect adjustment outside this tool to account for intra-cluster correlation.

faq.question: What is the difference between prospective and retrospective power analysis?
faq.answer: Prospective power analysis calculates the sample size needed before a study begins. This is the standard approach supported by this skill. Retrospective power analysis is not recommended and is not supported.

faq.question: How does the dropout adjustment work?
faq.answer: The dropout adjustment divides the calculated sample size by 1 minus the dropout rate. For example with 20 percent dropout, the target sample size increases by 25 percent.

faq.question: What statistical assumptions does this calculator make?
faq.answer: The calculator assumes normally distributed test statistics, equal variances for t-tests, proportional hazards for survival analysis, and uses normal approximation formulas throughout.

faq.question: Can I use this for non-inferiority trials?
faq.answer: Yes. Use the non-inferiority test type with the delta margin, sigma standard deviation, and alpha of 0.025 for a one-sided test. The calculator supports the basic formula for this design.
